acceptanceCriteria: A publicly accessible, documented list of canonical provider IDs exists that consumers can treat as a stable contract
A stability/deprecation policy is published: provider ID renames must go through a formal deprecation window where both old and new IDs are served, and changes are announced in a changelog
The `/providers.json` endpoint returns a `version` field whose value changes if and only if the provider catalog content changes
The `/data/voices.json` static artifact includes a `version` field whose value changes if and only if the voices catalog content changes
The `/api/v1/voices.json` live endpoint includes a `version` field whose value changes if and only if the voices catalog content changes

The version stamps on `/data/voices.json` and `/api/v1/voices.json` are identical when they derive from the same underlying source data, giving consumers confidence that both endpoints reflect the same catalog
outOfScope: How the version identifier is generated (hash, timestamp, ETag) — this is an implementation detail
Renaming or normalizing any existing provider IDs — the current IDs remain as-is
Changing the dispatch mechanism or alias-resolution logic
Backward compatibility beyond the deprecation window (once a deprecated ID's window closes, it may be removed)
Consumer-side conformance tooling or diffing utilities — that belongs in the consumer's codebase
Versioning any catalog artifact not listed above
Guarantees about voice data payload stability — only provider identity and catalog versioning are in scope
problemStatement: Downstream consumers that depend on Vokda's provider IDs have no way to detect when those IDs change between releases. Provider IDs can shift silently — renamed, added, or removed — and the current passthrough-by-default alias behavior means a renamed ID causes synthesis failures with zero advance warning. Consumers must either blindly re-fetch and deep-diff catalog artifacts on every release, or discover breakage only when their users hit it.
userValue: Gives integration owners (especially BYOK consumers like Khaos Machine) a reliable, low-noise drift signal. Instead of manual diffing or silent breakage, they can cheaply poll a version stamp, know the provider surface changed, and re-run their conformance checks with confidence. The stability contract also means renames won't surprise anyone — they'll be announced, documented, and served in parallel during a deprecation window.
